title: Tarta de Santiago
subtitle: The Galician almond cake, gluten-free and dairy-free
excerpt: The Spanish almond cake of Galicia: nothing but ground almonds, eggs, sugar, and lemon zest, dusted with icing sugar through a Santiago cross stencil. Eight slices. Dense, moist, and fully gluten-free.
sourceNotes: Tarta de Santiago is documented as a regional Galician speciality from at least the nineteenth century, with recipes in pre-1928 Spanish household cookery texts and regional food writing. The simple almond-egg-sugar formula appears in pre-1928 Spanish domestic cookery literature and is referenced in early-twentieth-century travel writing about Galicia. The Santiago cross decoration is the traditional finishing mark for the cake, associated with the pilgrimage route to the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 days. Do not refrigerate; it dries out.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 180°C. The cake is done when the top is pale golden and a skewer comes out with just a few moist crumbs.
baking.decoratingTechnique: icing sugar dusted through a Santiago cross stencil

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake made primarily or entirely from ground almonds instead of wheat flour. Because almonds contain no gluten, the structure comes from the eggs alone. The result is denser and moister than a flour-based sponge, and it does not rise much. Almond cakes go stale more slowly than flour cakes because the natural oil in the almonds keeps the crumb moist. They are inherently gluten-free, though cross-contamination from processing is possible if the almonds are not certified gluten-free.

content.text: Tarta de Santiago is a Galician almond cake with only four ingredients:

, eggs, sugar, and lemon zest. No flour, no butter, no leavening. It is dense, moist, and rich in the way that a nut cake is: filling in small slices. Eight slices. Finished with a dusting of icing sugar; a Santiago cross stencil is traditional.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan (180°C conventional). Grease and base-line the tin with baking paper. Whisk the eggs and sugar together vigorously for 2 to 3 minutes until pale and slightly thickened. Stir in the lemon zest and the cinnamon if using. Fold in the ground almonds until fully combined. The batter will be quite thick. Scrape into the tin and level the surface.

Bake for 28 to 32 minutes, until the top is pale golden brown and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out with only moist crumbs. The cake will feel firm rather than springy; almond cakes do not rise much. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and cool completely. Cut a Santiago cross shape from card, lay it on the cooled cake, and dust generously with icing sugar. Remove the stencil carefully.
